topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red ink swam on the paper, a damning indictment of my efforts. A C- in Advanced Calculus. I stared at the grade, my jaw tightening until my molars ached. It felt like a personal slight, a deliberate miscalculation on the part of Professor Albright. I picked up the syllabus again, tracing the lines of the grading rubric as if searching for some hidden loophole, a justification for this abysmal result. When Albright finally called my name during office hours, his tone was measured, almost detached, which only amplified the sting of his disappointment. "Your understanding of the theorems is superficial, Mark," he stated, tapping the sheet. I kept my hands clasped tightly in front of me, refusing to meet his gaze, letting the silence stretch until it felt suffocating. I just nodded once, a small, jerky movement, and retreated, the weight of that low mark settling heavily in my stomach.

[story 2]

I reread the feedback. "Insufficient depth in theoretical application." The words were sterile, academic, yet they landed like physical blows. My chest felt tight, a strange pressure building behind my ribs that made it hard to draw a full breath. I slammed the textbook shut, the sound echoing disproportionately loud in the quiet library corner. This wasn't a reflection of my intelligence; it felt like a deliberate slight against the hours I’d poured into those late nights, the complex problem sets I’d wrestled with. When I approached the TA the next day, my voice was sharper than intended. "I genuinely don't see how this assessment aligns with the material covered in week four," I managed, my knuckles white where I gripped my notebook. The TA looked apologetic, shuffling papers nervously, but I felt only a hardening resolve, a distinct refusal to accept this assessment as final.

[story 3]

The notification popped up on my screen: 68%. I didn't move for a full minute, just sitting there, the glow of the monitor illuminating the sudden flush creeping up my neck. It was absurd. I had studied relentlessly, sacrificed weekends, and yet this number stood as the final verdict. A low, almost inaudible sound escaped my throat—a low growl of disbelief. I ran a hand roughly through my hair, messing up the carefully combed strands, a purely reflexive gesture of agitation. I walked out of the lecture hall as if propelled by an unseen force, the hallway noise suddenly grating. I couldn't look at anyone; the entire environment seemed to mock my failure. I walked towards the exit, every step feeling heavy, burdened by the sheer injustice of the outcome.
